Brief Hospital Course:
Mrs. ___ was admitted to the neurology ICU for monitoring 
following a seizure and new findings of multiple intracranial 
hemorrhages on non-contrast head CT. After close monitoring, she 
was transferred to the neurology floor for further care.

#NEUROLOGY
She was placed on telemetry monitoring with seizure precautions, 
blood pressure was kept under 160 with her home blood pressure 
medications. She was contined on Keppra 1g BID, and she had no 
further seizures. This was decreased to 750mg BID and the 
patient remained clinically seizure-free. Extended routine EEG 
was normal. 

Repeat NCHCT showed stable hemorrhages. CTA was unremarkable. 
MRI unable to be performed due to pacemaker. Differential for 
her bleeds included amyloid angiopathy, metastatic disease, or 
emboli which could potentially be clot vs septic. For further 
workup, TTE was performed which was unchanged from prior with no 
specific embolic source such as a thrombus or vegetation found, 
and CT torso with and without contrast done which showed 
potentially suspicious cystic lesions in the kidney. For further 
workup, renal ultrasound was ordered. This showed that these 
lesions were most likely benign. Potential serum tumor markers 
were evaluated and were also normal. The leading diagnosis for 
the etiology of the brain hemorrhages is therefore amyloid 
angiopathy.

#CARDIOLOGY - she was continued on all of her home medications. 
TTE as above. Telemetry monitoring with atrial fibrillation. 
Warfarin was stopped and the patient and her family were 
counseled that given the high risk for recurrent brain 
hemorrhage, this should not be restarted in the future. She 
should start aspirin 81mg daily 1 week after the incident, which 
is ___. 

#ID - infectious workup, including CBC, UA, and CXR were 
negative. TTE with no evidence of endocarditis. She remained 
afebrile during her stay with no signs or symptoms of infection.

#Physical therapy saw the patient during her stay and on day of 
discharge recommended home with home ___. She will be going home 
with her daughter, who assures us that she will also have 24 
hour supervision.

___ was a pleasure caring for you during your stay. You were 
admitted to the hospital after new partial motor seizure, likely 
due to new multiple small spots of bleeding in the brain. The 
cause of the bleeding is uncertain, however we believe the most 
likely diagnosis is amyloid angiopathy. Other possibilities 
include bleeding from underlying neoplasm, or cardioembolic 
septic or clot emboli. We evaluated these possibilities with a 
CT torso and heart echocardiogram, neither of which showed any 
new concerning findings.

Given the new seizures, please continue Keppra (aka

Discharge Instructions:
Please follow the recommendations below to ensure a speedy and 
uneventful recovery.
 
ACTIVITY:
- Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
- You may climb stairs.
- You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until 
you see your surgeon at your next visit.
- Don't lift more than 10 lbs for 6 weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
- You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
- You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
- Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first.

HOW YOU MAY FEEL:
- You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
- You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
- You might have trouble concentrating or difficulty sleeping. 
You might feel somewhat depressed.
- You could have a poor appetite for a while. Food may seem 
unappealing.
- All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time. If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
- Your incision may be slightly red around the edges. This is 
normal.
- If you have steri strips, do not remove them for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that are on your incision.) But 
if they fall off before that that's okay).
- You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
- It is normal to feel a firm ridge along the incision. This 
will go away.
- Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
- Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
- You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid 
staining your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, 
please call your surgeon.
- You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
- Over the next ___ months, your incision will fade and become 
less prominent.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
- Constipation is a common side effect of medicine such as 
Percocet or codeine. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk 
of magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
- If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon.
- After some operations, diarrhea can occur. If you get 
diarrhea, don't take anti-diarrhea medicines. Drink plenty of 
fluitds and see if it goes away. If it does not go away, or is 
severe and you feel ill, please call your surgeon.
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness".
- Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
-You will receive a prescription from your surgeon for pain 
medicine to take by mouth. It is important to take this medicine 
as directed.
- Do not take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take 
more medicine at one time than prescribed.
- Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
- Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
- If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
- Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you 
cough or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
- Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just 
as you did before, unless you have been told differently.
- If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not 
to take, please call your surgeon.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ y/o F with hx of chronic postprandial pain presenting with 
likely acute pancreatitis.  
 
#Acute Pancreatitis/RUQ pain: In the setting of chronic 
postprandial RUQ pain, elevated lipase to 395, history of acute 
n/v, and radiologic findings (parapancreatic fat stranding, 
pancretic calcifications), acute pancreatitis was thought to be 
the most likely diagnosis. She received 3L of fluid in the ED, 
and was normotensive/non-tachycardic on the floor, producing 
adequate urine output. Given the history of chronic postprandial 
RUQ pain, gallstone pancreatitis was thought to be the most 
likely underlying etiology, with the stone having "passed" by 
the time of RUQ U/S. However, AP and Tbili were not elevated (86 
and 0.2, respectively), and the complete lack of findings (e.g. 
biliary sludge, mild CBD dilatation) on RUQ is atypical for 
gallstone pancreatitis. Possible other etiologies included 
drug-induced causes (namenda, donepezil, and celecoxib all have 
very rare (<1%) associations with pancreatits). Finally, her CT 
abd/pelvis was consistent with acute on chronic pancreatitis, 
and several cysts were visualized (though it was noted to be a 
suboptimal study for pancreatic visualization). Irritation from 
cystic material may have also been involved in her presentation. 
In any case, her ___ criteria score on admission was ___, 
consistent with mild pancreatitis, with an extremely low 
mortality (<5%). By the morning following admission, her lipase 
had dropped to 29 suggesting resolution of her pancreatitis and 
she was able to tolerate po intake. She will follow-up with GI 
as an outpatient for further evaluation of her pancreatic cysts 
with likely endoscopic ultrasound. 

#LLQ pain: LLQ pain was concerning for diverticulitis vs 
constipation. Notably, she does not complain of pain at rest, 
which is surpising in the context of diverticulitis. Her CT did 
not show any signs of diverticulitis or inflammation in LLQ. 
Further, she did have a leukocytosis and was afebrile, and has 
symptomatically improved on NPO and fluids alone--this is not 
typical of diverticulitis. Given that thse has not had a BM in 3 
days, it is thus likely that this pain is related to 
constipation. Her pain improved after bowel movement. Patient 
takes narcotics as an outpatient and she will be placed on 
laxative regimen. 

#Right leg swelling, calf pain: Given the history of an 
athroscopic R knee procedure in ___, it is possible 
that the R leg swelling compared with left leg swelling is her 
baseline. However, given her history of left ankle sprain (and 
thus possibility of impaired mobility for the past month) and 
her complaint of right calf pain, there was a possibility ___ 
DVT, but ___ US was negative. 

#L ankle pain: Patient complained of point tenderness over the 
left lateral ankle. She had a fall in 2 months ago and an ankle 
film one month ago that did not show any fracture. As she has 
point tenderness, another x-ray was performed. It showed 
sclerosis at the distal tibia but no abnormalities at the site 
of her pain. She was also able to tolerate standing without pain 
and no further imaging was needed.

to 136. Her urine sodium was 185 and her UOsm was 547. Her 
calculated serum osmolarity, on the other hand, was 276.2. If 
she were intravascularly depleted, this could be considered 
appropriate. Her sodium status improved after volume repletion.

#HTN: She was continued on atenolol 25 mg po BID (which was from

told us she took atenolol 25 mg po daily and diovan 80 mg po 
daily and she was restarted on this regimen at discharge.

osteophytes resulting in bilateral mild neural foraminal narrowing.

narrowing.

posterior vertebral osteophytes, uncovertebral and facet joint osteophytes

1.  Type 2 odontoid fracture.  Given the STIR signal abnormality at the site

findings.

#NUTRITION
Patient with chronic MRSA-infected ulcerations of the

She was deemed to have at least 3 risk factors, corresponding to

This is a ___ year old ___ female with past medical

# Acute hypoxic respiratory failure secondary to acute asthma 
exacerbation 
Patient presented with hypoxia and hypercarbia requiring 
intubation upon arrival to the ICU. Treated with solumedrol, 
bronchodilators. Transitioned to prednisone with taper given 
severity of asthma exacerbation. Able to be extubated after 2 
days on the ventilator. Trigger thought to be due to viral 
illness from her nursing home. She finished 3 days of IV 
methylprednisone (___), followed by prednisone taper 
started ___ (40mg PO x2 days, then 20mg PO x2 days, then 10mg PO 
x2 days) and nebs. Also received Singulair (10 mg PO DAILY) as 
well as Advair 1 INH IH BID. Tolerated changing standing to PRN 
levalbuterol and ipratropium to tiopropium which can be 
continued at rehab. By report from rehab, she was intermittently 
on O2 as an outpatient prior to admission. On discharge she was 
mainly off O2 during the day but intermittently required O2 at 
night, thought to either be atelectasis or undiagnosed OSA. 

# Acute L brachial vein DVT - Found on US ___ in left brachial, 
as left hand was swollen. ___ records did not show that she had 
CVL during prior hospitalization to explain the DVT. Patient 
started on heparin gtt without bolus after consulting neurology 
on risk/benefit of recent hemorrhagic stroke (see below). Family 
meeting held to discuss risks and benefits to anticoagulation. 
Family agreed with treating DVT (and she did not undergo a CTA 
to look for PE given improvement in respiratory status and plan 
for anticoagulation for DVT). Heparin gtt (___), followed 
by Lovenox bridge (___) to Warfarin (___). Now at INR 1.7 
on 5mg warfarin.

# NSTEMI
# Takotsubo stress cardiomyopathy 
Acute elevation of troponin peaking at 0.8 with concomitant TWI 
in anterolateral leads. Echo consistent with Takatsubo's likely 
from stress of asthma exacerbation. Management was targeted 
towards reducing cardiac demand by
putting her on mechanical ventilation, metoprolol, and losartan. 
Did not get fluids or inotropes. Cardiology did not recommend 
ASA or increasing statin given low suspicion for ACS.  

# Dementia with behavioral disturbance
# Stroke with residual L partial hemiparesis and dysphagia

ambulate. Has left sided weakness, but able to move hand with 
weak squeeze and move left foot. Was lethargic in setting of 
acute illness above.  CT head without acute processes. No change 
after starting anticoagulation as above.  Mental status improved 
to recent baseline of A&O x1.

[2128-03-09 00:02:00] RR: INDICATION:  Acute nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea with a white blood cell
count of 35.

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial MDCT images were obtained through the abdomen and
pelvis after the administration of oral Gastrografin only.  IV contrast was
not administered due to elevated creatinine.  Sagittal and coronal reformatted
images were obtained and reviewed.

FINDINGS:

LUNG BASES:  There is mild right plate-like basilar atelectasis.  There is no
consolidation, nodule, or pleural effusion.  The base of the heart is
unremarkable.  Atherosclerotic calcifications are noted along the aortic
valve.  There is no pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:  The liver is normal in shape and contour.  Within the limitations of
a non-contrast exam, there is no evidence of a focal renal lesion.  Air is
noted in the biliary system, which is expected after a known sphincterotomy. 
There is mild-to-moderate intrahepatic biliary duct dilation.  The gallbladder
is extremely distended with surrounding pericholecystic fluid.  Stones are
layering dependently within the gallbladder.  There is also nondependent
calcification superiorly (2, 27).  Additionally, there appears to be stones
within the common bile duct, although the duct anatomy is difficult to
distinguish in this non-contrast exam (2, 23).  The spleen is normal.  The
pancreas is atrophic without focal pancreatic lesions or duct dilation.  The
right adrenal gland is normal.  There is mild thickening of the left adrenal
gland without a discrete mass.  The kidneys are minimally atrophic with
nonspecific bilateral perinephric stranding, likely due to chronic kidney
disease.  In the left kidney, there is a 7-mm hypodense lesion which is too
small to fully characterize, but likely represents a cyst.  There is no
evidence of hydronephrosis.

The stomach is minimally distended and filled with oral contrast.  Contrast is
noted along the duodenum.  The space between the duodenum and the large
distended gallbladder is difficult to evaluate, although there appear to be
reactive inflammatory changes of the duodenum in the area where it is in
contact with the gallbladder.  The remainder of the small bowel is
unremarkable.  There is no free fluid.  There is no free air.  There is no
mesenteric, abdominal, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.  Small scattered
lymph nodes are present, although do not meet criteria for pathologic
enlargement.

The abdominal vasculature is normal in caliber.  Moderate atherosclerotic
disease is present.

PELVIS:  There is a small amount of stranding along a portion of the left
descending colon with an air-filled diverticulum (2, 31).  The remainder of
the large bowel is unremarkable.  There is some nonhemorrhagic free fluid in
the pelvis.  The uterus and adnexa are unremarkable.  There is no pelvic or
inguinal lymphadenopathy.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES:  There are no concerning lytic or sclerotic osseous
lesions.  Severe degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine.  There is
a probable sebaceous cyst present posteriorly in the back (602B, 49).

IMPRESSION:
1.  Massively distended gallbladder with multiple stones, surrounding
stranding, and free fluid in the abdomen and pelvis.  This is consistent with
complicated acute calculus cholecystitis.
2.  Mild-to-moderate intrahepatic biliary duct dilation.
3.  Probable stones within the common bile duct, although difficult to
evaluate on this non-contrast exam.  Recommend right upper quadrant ultrasound
for further evaluation.
4.  Nonhemorrhagic free fluid in the pelvis, likely extending from the
inflammatory changes in the abdomen from acute cholecystitis.
5.  Focal stranding around the left descending colon is likely mild
diverticulitis.
6.  Pneumobilia, which is expected after a sphincterotomy.

Discharge Instructions
Brain Hemorrhage with Surgery

Brain Surgery

· You underwent a surgery called a craniectomy. A portion of 
your skull was removed to allow your brain to swell. You then 
underwent cranioplasty, which replaced the bone. You no longer 
need to wear a helmet when out of bed. 

It is best to keep your incision open to air but it is ok to 
cover it when outside. 

· Call your ___ if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage. 

Spine Surgery - Spinal Fusion

· Your incision is closed with staples . You will need staple 
removal. 

· Do not apply any lotions or creams to the site. 

· Please keep your incision dry until removal of your staple

· Please avoid swimming for two weeks after staple removal.

· Call your ___ if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage. 

Activity

· You do not need any bracing or helmets at this time. 

· We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, 
or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up appointment.

· You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace once you are symptom free at rest. 
___ try to do too much all at once.

· No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. 

· If you experienced a seizure while admitted, you are NOT 
allowed to drive by law. 

· No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. You 
should avoid contact sports for 6 months. 

· Do  NOT smoke. Smoking can affect your healing and fusion.

Medications

· Do not take any anti-inflammatory medications such as Motrin, 
Advil, Aspirin, and Ibuprofen etc until cleared by your 
neurosurgeon. This can interfere with your spine fusion.

· You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.

 
What You ___ Experience:

· You may have difficulty paying attention, concentrating, and 
remembering new information.

· Emotional and/or behavioral difficulties are common. 

· Feeling more tired, restlessness, irritability, and mood 
swings are also common.

· You may also experience some post-operative swelling around 
your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the second and third day of surgery.  You apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to your eyes to help with the 
swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after 
laying flat from sleeping but decrease when up. 

· You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal from 
the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 

· Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids and 
eat a high-fiber diet. If you are taking narcotics (prescription 
pain medications), try an over-the-counter stool softener.

Headaches:

· Headache is one of the most common symptoms after a brain 
bleed. 

· Most headaches are not dangerous but you should call your 
doctor if the headache gets worse, develop arm or leg weakness, 
increased sleepiness, and/or have nausea or vomiting with a 
headache. 

· Mild pain medications may be helpful with these headaches but 
avoid taking pain medications on a daily basis unless prescribed 
by your doctor. 

· There are other things that can be done to help with your 
headaches: avoid caffeine, get enough sleep, daily exercise, 
relaxation/ meditation, massage, acupuncture, heat or ice packs.

Discharge Instructions
Brain Hemorrhage with Surgery

Surgery
You underwent a surgery called a craniotomy to have blood 
removed from your brain. 
Please keep your sutures and staple along your incision dry 
until they are removed.
It is best to keep your incision open to air but it is ok to 
cover it when outside. 
Call your surgeon if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage. 

Activity
We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, 
or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up appointment.
You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace once you are symptom free at rest. 
___ try to do too much all at once.
No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. 
If you experienced a seizure while admitted, you are NOT 
allowed to drive by law. 
No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. You 
should avoid contact sports for 6 months. 

Medications
Please do NOT take any blood thinning medication (Aspirin, 
Ibuprofen, Plavix, Coumadin) until cleared by the neurosurgeon. 
You have been discharged on Keppra (Levetiracetam) for 6 
additional days. This medication helps to prevent seizures. 
Please continue this medication as indicated on your discharge 
instruction. It is important that you take this medication 
consistently and on time. 
You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.

What You ___ Experience:
You may have difficulty paying attention, concentrating, and 
remembering new information.
Emotional and/or behavioral difficulties are common. 
Feeling more tired, restlessness, irritability, and mood 
swings are also common.
You may also experience some post-operative swelling around 
your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the second and third day of surgery.  You apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to your eyes to help with the 
swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after 
laying flat from sleeping but decrease when up. 
You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal from 
the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 
Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids and 
eat a high-fiber diet. If you are taking narcotics (prescription 
pain medications), try an over-the-counter stool softener.

Headaches:
Headache is one of the most common symptoms after a brain 
bleed. 
Most headaches are not dangerous but you should call your 
doctor if the headache gets worse, develop arm or leg weakness, 
increased sleepiness, and/or have nausea or vomiting with a 
headache. 
Mild pain medications may be helpful with these headaches but 
avoid taking pain medications on a daily basis unless prescribed 
by your doctor. 
There are other things that can be done to help with your 
headaches: avoid caffeine, get enough sleep, daily exercise, 
relaxation/ meditation, massage, acupuncture, heat or ice packs.

site.

Weakness or changes in sensation in your face, arms, or leg

Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, or loss of balance or

PTT of 50-60 given the prior bleeding. She tolerated this well. 
She was also transfused a single unit of packed red blood cells 
for a HCT of 24% this admission, with good response. An active 
type and screen was maintained, IV access was maintained and 
Protonix 40 mg IV Q12 was continued until bleeding had resolved. 
Hemodynamics were monitored via telemetry. Serial hematocrits 
were monitored closely. A Foley catheter was maintained until 
her volume status stabilized and bleeding issues resolved. She 
will continue with the Foley on discharge.
.

in ___ now with prolonged immobilization and mostly 
wheelchair-bound. The patinet had a CT chest this admission 
without evidence of acute PE but it was without contrast given 
renal issues. The patient was admitted on Coumadin at home 
(admitted with a subtherapeutic INR of 1.5-1.6) but was not 
taking doses. She was then recently heparinized given PE and 
NSTEMI concerns this admission, and was found to have LLE clot 
burden in her common and superficial femoral veins on 
ultrasound. She was continued on a heparin gtt with goal PTT 
50-60 and frequent PTT checks. She was maintained on SCDs this 
admission. She had warm compresses applied to her left lower 
thigh given pain from the clot. A hip and femur X-ray was 
performed on the left side given on-going pain, which showed no 
acute fracture or malalignment. She did have a calcified fibroid 
seen on the pelvic film, which is likely symptomatic. The source 
of her recurrent DVT/PE disease was not fully elucidated. She 
has a prior DVT in ___, and has been on Coumadin. She will 
continue on Coumadin as an outpatient with INR monitoring (goal 
___.
.

single blood culture positive from admission grew coagulase 
negative Staph which was likely a skin contaminent. Her WBC on 
admission was 13 and given her tenuous medical status, empiric 
Vancomycin was continued for treatment over 2-days (renally 
dosed); but this was discontinued when no further cultures were 
positive. She remained afebrile and her WBC was attributed to 
UTI (see below). 
.

135/80 given her diabetes and PVD, along with her CAD risks. She 
remained normotensive for most of this admission with medical 
optimization. She was managed with beta-blocker, nitrate and 
hydralazine. She had some labile systolic hypertension issues 
and required transitioning to long-acting blood pressure 
medications. An ACEI was avoided given her renal function.
.
# ANEMIA/THROMBOCYTOPENIA - The patient was admitted with a HCT 
27.9 which was later noted to be 31.6. Following her heparin gtt 
bleeding issues, her HCT reached a nadir of 24.1% and she was 
transfused 1 units of packed red cells with improvement. The 
source of hematocrit decline was likely her heparin bleeding 
issues, but no recent colonoscopy was noted in her records. Her 
platelets were 218 on admission and these dropped to 138 this 
admission; this was closely monitored with no evidence of 
petechiae or worsening bleeding concerns. Her transfusion goal 
was set as a HCT > 26% given her likely coronary disease. We 
continued monitoring her serial HCTs, hemodynamics and trended 
her platelets. She likly needs age appropriate screening given 
her hematocrit trend. This was discussed with her HCP who felt 
invasive screening should be avoided if possible.
.
# TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (IDDM) - The patient has a diagnosis 
of diabetes that requires insulin, diagnosed in her ___. She has 
been maintained on Inuslin NPH/regular at home 28 units in the 
AM and 12 units in the ___ with sliding scale as needed. She had 
no reported HbA1c for comparison, but this admission it was 
7.2%. The patient was monitored with blood glucoses in the range 
of 200 mg/dL. We continued her insulin once she tolerated diet 
and this was increased to 30 units in the AM and 14 units in the 
evening of her NPH insulin regimen with good effect. She had no 
hypoglycemic episodes. We continue Q6H glucose monitoring.
.

admitted with known chronic renal insufficiency likely a result 
of hypertension and diabetic nephropathy. Her baseline 
creatinine is in the range of 1.3 from her records (admission 
2.3). She was admitted with a creatinine elevation of 2.3 which 
was attributed to poor renal perfusion and systolic dysfunction 
versus the effects of diuresis; likely all pre-renal in 
etiology. Her medications were renally dosed, nephrotoxins were 
avoided and her creatinine improved to 1.7 prior to discharge. 
.
# ACUTE MENTAL STATUS CHANGES - The patient developed visual 
hallucinations on ___ she was given Zyprexa 2.5 mg PO x 1 with 
some effect but it was felt that her poor renal clearance 
contributed to a metabolic encephalopathy versus poor medication 
clearance which caused her mental status issues. We considered 
an infectious etiology, for which she was treated for UTI, she 
had negative blood cultures and no consolidation on CXR. Her 
neuro exam was without evidence of TIA/stroke. Her renal 
function was likely contributing to metabolic encephalopathy, 
and she improved with her improvement in renal function. 
Gabapentin, Vicodin and other deliriogenic medications were held 
with her mental status changes and slowly re-titrated with close 
monitoring. She had an ABG for a low suspicion of hypoxia on

On HD2 his hematocrit continued to trend down and he was 
transfused with 2 units of packed red blood cells. On HD3 his 
hematocrit continued to trend down and he received 2 more units 
of packed red blood cells. Interventional radiology was 
reconsulted and preformed a successful Gel-Foam embolization of 
the posterior division of the left internal iliac artery. His 
hematocrit levels remained stable.

___ Diabetic Center was consulted for high glucose in the 
setting of elevated creatinine and recommended transitioning to 
Lantus, glipizide, and Januvia with further outpatient 
monitoring of his creatinine and glucose.

hypotension to the 60's systolic. His hematocrit was stable, 
troponins were negative for elevation, his ECG was baseline 
atrial fibrillation. He received 1 Liter of Lactated Ringers and 
his symptoms improved and was hemodynamically stable. He 
received 1 unit of packed red blood cells for suspected 
decreased oxygen carrying capacity in setting of anemia with 
history of heart failure. On HD8, he was initially feeling well 
but had an episode of weakness/dizziness improved with sitting

decreasing his lisinopril dose. He had subsequent improvement of 
symptoms.

PROCEDURE:
1. Right common femoral artery access.
2. Left internal iliac arteriogram.
3. Posterior division left internal iliac artery arteriogram.
4. Gel-Foam embolization of posterior division of the left internal iliac
artery.

PROCEDURE DETAILS:
Following the discussion of the risks, benefits and alternatives to the
procedure, written informed consent was obtained from the patient. The patient
was then brought to the angiography suite and placed supine on the exam table.
A pre-procedure time-out was performed per ___ protocol. Both groins were
prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.

Using palpatory and fluoroscopic guidance, the right common femoral artery was
punctured using a micropuncture set at the level of the mid-femoral head. A
0.018 wire was passed easily into the vessel lumen. A small skin incision was
made over the needle. Then the inner dilator and wire were removed and a
___ wire was advanced under fluoroscopy into the aorta. The micropuncture
sheath was exchanged for a 5 ___ sheath which was attached to a continuous
heparinized saline side arm flush.

A Omni Flush catheter was advanced over ___ wire into the aorta.  The
___ wire was exchanged for a Glidewire and the Glidewire was advanced down
the left external iliac artery.  The Omni Flush catheter was exchanged for a
pudendal catheter, which was formed over the aortic bifurcation and advanced
to the left iliac bifurcation, where an arteriogram was performed.  Next, the
posterior division of the left internal iliac artery was selected with the
pudendal catheter and an arteriogram was performed.  At this point, the
decision was made to embolize the enteric posterior division of the left
internal iliac artery with Gel-Foam.

Gel-Foam embolization was performed to near stasis.  Repeat hand injection of
the posterior division of the left internal iliac artery demonstrate reflux of
contrast into the anterior division of the left internal iliac artery, and
substantially reduced antegrade flow.  The pudendal catheter was removed over
a wire, which is also removed.  The 5 ___ sheath was removed.

An Angioseal closure device was deployed and manual pressure was held until
hemostasis was achieved. Sterile dressings were applied. The patient tolerated
the procedure well.

1.  Left internal iliac artery angiogram and posterior division of the
internal iliac artery angiogram demonstrating no evidence of active
extravasation, pseudoaneurysm, or vessel irregularity to suggest the source of
active arterial bleeding.
2.  Gel-Foam embolization of the posterior division of the left internal iliac
artery to near stasis.
3.  Post embolization arteriogram demonstrating no significant antegrade flow
in the posterior division of the left internal iliac artery.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. ___ was admitted to ICU s/p Cardiac Arrest.  At time of 
transfer to ___, he was intubated and requiring 2 pressors.  
In the ___ ED, his central line from OSH was pulled and new 
Right IJ placed.  Pressors were changed to levophed and 
phenylephrine.  Cooling pads were placed in the ED and actualy 
had to be warmed to 33 degrees C for the cooling protocol.  On 
arrival to the MICU, patient appeared to be in normal sinus 
rhythm with blood pressure in systolic 80's.  Levophed and 
phenylephrine were at maximum doses.  He was administered IVF 
for resuscitation.  Labs were notable for worsening acidosis, 
started on bicarb gtt pending further studies.  Vent changed to 
increase ventilation in effort to improve acidosis.  Patient 
began to ooze from all line sites, most notably the right 
?subclavian site from OSH line that was pulled in the ED.  DIC 
labs were positive, he was given FFP and cryopreciptate with 
minimal improvement.  Surgery was contacted given worsening 
bleeding and hematoma at right line site - recommended applying 
continued pressure.  Hypotension continued to worsen, requiring 
continuous IVF at maximum rates.  Oxygenation was maintained 
with mechanical ventilation on the vent.  After discussion with 
ex-wife and step daughter, were able to contact patient's father 
and brother.  After discussion of clinical course and prognosis, 
decision was made to withdraw care and make comfort measures.  
All medications were stopped and patient passed away at 5:05 am 
on ___.  Medical Examiner accepted the case.
